title: Exhaustive and feasible parametrisation with applications to the travelling salesperson problem
authors: Marvin Schwiering, Timo Ziegler, Lennart Binkowski, Benjamin Sambale
abstract: This paper introduces the concept of exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuits for constrained combinatorial optimisation problems. Such circuits can reach, given the right parameter values, every feasible solution with certainty -- including the optimum -- with a fixed number of parameters, while avoiding infeasible solutions altogether. This is in sharp contrast to conventional quantum alternating operator ansatz schemes, which are merely guaranteed to reach the optimum asymptotically. We introduce an abstract pipeline for constructing exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting circuits from a transitive group action on a problem's feasible set. Our constructions rely on the simple combination of the group action with group representation and the novel notion of generating sequences: group elements in fixed order, possibly with repetitions, that generate the entire group. That is, we trace expressivity of parametrised quantum circuits back to the most fundamental concepts of group theory. We apply this pipeline to two concrete examples for the travelling salesperson problem, thus showing that exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting circuits are not an empty definition. Furthermore, we provide numerical proof-of-principles on instances with up to nine cities, comparing the suitability of our constructions for parameter optimisation purposes against established mixers.
summary: This paper introduces a new class of quantum circuits for combinatorial optimization that are guaranteed to reach all feasible solutions, including the optimum, without leaving the feasible subspace. By leveraging group theory and generating sequences of group elements, the authors provide a systematic way to build these circuits. They demonstrate that for the Traveling Salesperson Problem, a binary insertion-based construction significantly outperforms standard QAOA in numerical tests. This approach reduces the parameter space and improves the efficiency of the classical optimization process.
main_problem: Addressing the reachability and feasibility issues in QAOA, where conventional circuits may not cover all optimal solutions or may include infeasible states for constrained combinatorial optimization problems.
main_result: The authors develop a pipeline to construct 'exhaustively parameterized, feasibility-respecting' quantum circuits that are guaranteed to reach every feasible solution with a fixed number of parameters, demonstrating superior performance on TSP instances.
method: The method uses group theory, specifically identifying transitive group actions and generating sequences of involutions, to build parameterized unitaries that navigate the feasible subspace of a problem.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) modeled as an optimization over the symmetric group $S_n$, using both one-hot and compact qubit encodings.
key_observables: Approximation ratio (normalized expectation value of the objective Hamiltonian).
important_parameters: Number of variational parameters (scaling as $O(n^2)$ for bubble sort and $O(n \log n)$ for binary insertion) and the number of layers $p$.
main_assumptions: The numerical validation assumes noiseless qubits and focuses on small-scale instances (up to 9 cities); it does not guarantee that the required optimal parameters can be found efficiently.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the abstract pipeline; Figure 2 illustrates the circuit construction flow; Figure 3 shows the implementation of exponentiated involutions using an ancilla; Figure 4 shows qubit connectivity; Figure 5 compares approximation ratios vs. optimization iterations for different methods.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of exhaustive reachability, provides a group-theoretic mathematical framework (the abstract pipeline), applies this to TSP via bubble sort and binary insertion constructions, and concludes with numerical benchmarks and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. High-level overview of our abstract pipeline for constructing exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuits for combinatorial optimisation problems. The task of exactly and exclusively preparing all feasible solutions via paremtrised quantum circuits can be reduced to concepts of elementary group theory. The two key ingredients are a transitive group action on the problem’s feasible set, often available from decades of operational research studying symmetries in the feasible set, and a generating sequence of involutions. The output of the pipeline is an exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuit with a fixed number of parameters.

Fig. 2. Abstract pipeline for constructing an exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuit from a transitive group action on the feasible set F ⊂𝕓m. Central to this construction is the existence of a generating sequence of involutions, whose elements can be represented as m-qubit operators. Exponentiating these operators, introducing real parameters in the exponent, and prepending the state preparation of some feasible computational basis state |x⟩, completes the construction.

Fig. 4. Qubit connectivity required for implementing the exhaustively parametrised quantum circuit based on the bubble sort sequence. The m data qubits, encoding the TSP solutions, can be aligned on a chain. The ancilla qubit, serving as control for the Fredkin gates, has to be connected to all m data qubits. Alternatively, one can introduce m −1 ancilla qubits, each connected to two contiguous data qubits.

Fig. 5. Approximation ratios obtained after each COBYLA iteration, using different parametrised quantum circuits on a 9-city TSP instance. The QAOA with Hadfield et al.’s mixers was not able to significantly improve initial state’s approximation ratio, neither if a single computational basis state, nor the uniform superposition of all feasible states, was used. In contrast, the exhaustively parametrised circuit based on bubble sort was able to push the initial ratio of ≈0.60 to 0.83, while the construction based on the binary insertion sequence even reached a final approximation ratio of 0.91.
